--- format: revealjs: theme: ../../styles/meds-slides-styles.scss slide-number: true chalkboard: true title-slide: false jupyter: eds232-env --- ## {#title-slide data-menu-title="Title Slide" background="#053660"} [EDS 232]{.custom-title}
[Lesson 3]{.custom-subtitle} [*Simple linear regression*]{.custom-subtitle} --- ## {#in-this-lesson data-menu-title="In this lesson"} [In this lesson]{.slide-title}

- The simple linear regression (SLR) model - Estimating coefficients with least squares - Standard errors and confidence intervals - Hypothesis testing: $t$-statistic and $p$-values - Assessing model accuracy: RSE and $R^2$ --- ## {#why-linear-regression data-menu-title="Why study linear regression?"} [Why study linear regression?]{.slide-title}
Regression estimates relationships between predictors and a response. It can help answer questions such as:
1. Is there a relationship between the predictor(s) and the response? 2. How strong is that relationship? 3. Which predictors are actually associated with the response? 4. How large is the effect of each predictor on the response? 5. Is the relationship linear? 6. How accurately can we predict the response for a given combination of predictor values?
. . . ::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text} Linear regression supports both **inference** and **prediction**. ::: --- ## {#section-example data-menu-title="# A guiding example #" background="#047C90"}

A guiding example

--- ## {#greenabate data-menu-title="The GreenAbate dataset"} [The GreenAbate dataset]{.slide-title}
A research consortium tracks **200 manufacturing firms**. For each firm, they record annual spending on three emissions-reduction strategies and the resulting CO₂ savings:
::: {.body-text-m} - **`process`** — investment in cleaner production processes (\$K/year) - **`efficiency`** — investment in energy efficiency upgrades (\$K/year) - **`offsets`** — carbon offset purchases (\$K/year) - **`co2_reduction`** — annual CO₂ emissions reduced (ktCO₂e/year) :::
The researchers want to understand: ::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text} *Do these investments actually drive emissions reductions, and if so, by how much?* ::: --- ## {#greenabate-plots data-menu-title="GreenAbate: data overview"} [CO₂ reduction vs. each predictor]{.slide-title}
::: {#853f5caf .cell execution_count=2} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-3-output-1.png){width=1187 height=390 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: ::: {.center-text .body-text-s} *Synthetic data generated for educational purposes only* ::: . . . `process` spending: strongest linear association with CO₂ reductions. We will use this predictor as our variable today and go back to the others in the next lesson. --- ## {#section-slr data-menu-title="# Simple linear regression #" background="#047C90"}

Simple linear regression

--- ## {#slr-model data-menu-title="The SLR model"} [The simple linear regression model]{.slide-title}
**Simple linear regression (SLR)** assumes a linear relationship between a single predictor $X$ and a response $Y$: $$Y \approx \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$$ . . . ::: {.body-text-m} - $\beta_0 =$ **$y$-intercept**: expected value of $Y$ when $X = 0$ - $\beta_1 =$ **slope**: average change in $Y$ for a one-unit increase in $X$ ::: . . .
Since $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ are unknown, we **estimate** them from data: $$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x = \hat{f}(x)$$ --- ## {#slr-greenabate data-menu-title="SLR: fitted line"} [Using a linear model for our data]{.slide-title}
Using `process` as the sole predictor, a linear model for our data would assume this functional form: $$\texttt{co2_reduction} \approx \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 \cdot \texttt{process}$$ ::: {#ee7a19f4 .cell execution_count=3} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-4-output-1.png){width=789 height=340 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: --- ## {#linear-model-checkin data-menu-title="Check-in"} [Check in ]{.slide-title}
:::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} - How would you interpret the value of $\hat{\beta}_0$? - How would you interpret the value of $\hat{\beta}_1$? - If a firm spends \$150K on process upgrades, how would you calculate the predicted CO₂ reduction? ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {#b8c4fa49 .cell execution_count=4} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-5-output-1.png){width=709 height=390 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: ::: :::: --- ## {#linear-model-checkin-answers data-menu-title="Check-in Answers"} [Check in ]{.slide-title}
:::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} - How would you interpret the value of $\hat{\beta}_0$? - How would you interpret the value of $\hat{\beta}_1$? - If a firm spends \$150K on process upgrades, how would you calculate the predicted CO₂ reduction?
- $\hat{\beta}_0 \approx$ 7\.70 ktCO₂e: predicted CO₂ reduction when a firm spends \$0 on process upgrades (baseline). - $\hat{\beta}_1 \approx$ 0\.0460 ktCO₂e per \$K: each additional \$1K in process spending is associated with 0\.0460 ktCO₂e of additional reduction. - Just plug in 150 into the line equation! ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {#23ccacf0 .cell execution_count=5} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-6-output-1.png){width=709 height=390 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: ::: :::: --- ## {#section-estimation data-menu-title="# Estimating the coefficients #" background="#047C90"}

Estimating the coefficients

--- ## {#residuals-def data-menu-title="Residuals and RSS"} [Residuals and the RSS]{.slide-title}
Given $n$ training observations $(x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$, we want to find $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ such that the line $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x$ is as close as possible to the observed data. . . . The **residual** for observation $i$ is the difference between observed and predicted: $$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = y_i - \hat{\beta}_0 - \hat{\beta}_1 x_i$$ . . . The **residual sum of squares (RSS)** totals all squared residuals: $$RSS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{\beta}_0 - \hat{\beta}_1 x_i)^2.$$ . . .
::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text} The **least squares** approach to fit the regression line chooses $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ to **minimize** the RSS. ::: --- ## {#residuals-plot data-menu-title="Residuals: visual"} [Residuals can be visualized as the vertical distance to the fitted line]{.slide-title}
::: {#71df9e67 .cell execution_count=6} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-7-output-1.png){width=789 height=390 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: --- ## {#ls-formulas data-menu-title="Least squares formulas"} [Least squares solution]{.slide-title}
Minimizing the RSS using calculus gives the following formulas:
$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$
$$\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x}$$ . . .
- The estimates depend entirely on the training data: if you feed the model different data, you will get different estimates. This is the source of estimation uncertainty. - In practice, **the computer computes these** for you. --- ## {#section-accuracy data-menu-title="# Coefficient accuracy #" background="#047C90"}

Accuracy of the coefficient estimates

--- ## {#standard-errors data-menu-title="Standard errors"} [Standard errors]{.slide-title}
Our true function describing the relationship between predictor and response is given by $f(X) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$. Our observed data is described by: $$Y = f(X) + \epsilon = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \epsilon,$$ The **standard error** quantifies how far $\hat{\beta}_i$ is from the true $\beta_i$ on average. The standard error for the slope $\hat{\beta}_1$ is: $$SE(\hat{\beta}_1)^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$ The standard error of the $y$-intercept $\hat{\beta}_0$ is: $$SE(\hat{\beta}_0)^2 = \sigma^2 \left[\frac{1}{n} + \frac{\bar{x}^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}\right]$$ . . . ::: {.body-text-s} $\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(\epsilon)$ is the error variance (unknown in practice — estimated from the data). ::: **Check-in:** In these formulas, what do $n$, $x_i$, $\bar{x}$ represent? --- ## {#confidence-intervals data-menu-title="Confidence intervals"} [Confidence intervals]{.slide-title}
A **95% confidence interval** for a coefficient $\beta_i$ is: $$[\hat{\beta}_i - 2 \cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_i),\;\; \hat{\beta}_i + 2 \cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_i)]$$ ::: {.body-text-s} *(the factor 2 is approximate; the exact value comes from the $t$-distribution with $n-2$ d.f.)* ::: . . .
::: {.body-text-m} **Interpretation:** > A 95% CI for $\beta_1$ of $[a_1, b_1]$ means there is a 95% chance that a one-unit increase in $x$ is associated with an average increase in $y$ between $a_1$ and $b_1$ units. > A 95% CI for $\beta_0$ of $[a_0, b_0]$ means there is a 95% chance that the regression line crosses the $y$-axis between $a_0$ and $b_0$. ::: --- ## {#ci-greenabate data-menu-title="CIs: GreenAbate"} [95% confidence intervals for the GreenAbate SLR]{.slide-title}
::: {#c97f1296 .cell execution_count=7} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 2.5% 97.5% Intercept 6.8234 8.5672 process 0.0408 0.0511 ``` ::: ::: . . .
::: {.body-text-m} - The 95% CI for $\beta_1$ (process) is $[0\.041,\; 0\.051]$ — entirely above 0. - Strong evidence that higher process spending is associated with greater CO₂ reductions. ::: --- ## {#checkin-ci data-menu-title="Check-in: confidence intervals" background="#053660"}

Check-in

The 95% CI for $\beta_1$ is $[0\.041,\; 0\.051]$.

What if the interval had been $[-0.002,\; 0\.051]$ instead?

What would that tell us about the relationship?

--- ## {#section-hypothesis data-menu-title="# Hypothesis testing #" background="#047C90"}

Hypothesis testing

--- ## {#hypotheses data-menu-title="Null and alternative hypotheses"} [Is there a relationship between $X$ and $Y$?]{.slide-title}

::: {.body-text-m} - **Null hypothesis** $H_0$: $\beta_1 = 0$ — no relationship between $X$ and $Y$ → $\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta}_0$ (a constant; $X$ carries no information) - **Alternative hypothesis** $H_a$: $\beta_1 \neq 0$ — there is a relationship → $\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 X$ ::: . . .
::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text} To test $H_0$, we need to determine whether $\hat{\beta}_1$ is sufficiently far from 0 relative to its uncertainty. ::: --- ## {#t-stat data-menu-title="t-statistic and p-value"} [The $t$-statistic and $p$-value]{.slide-title}
The **$t$-statistic** measures how many standard deviations $\hat{\beta}_1$ is from 0: $$t = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1}{SE(\hat{\beta}_1)}$$ . . . The **$p$-value** is the probability of observing $|t|$ this large, *assuming $H_0$ is true*. . . .
::: {.body-text-m} - **Small $p$-value** ($p < 0.05$): unlikely to see $\hat{\beta}_1$ this far from 0 by chance → **reject $H_0$** → evidence of a relationship - **Large $p$-value**: data are consistent with $\beta_1 = 0$ → **fail to reject $H_0$** ::: --- ## {#regression-table data-menu-title="Regression table"} [SLR output: `co2_reduction` ~ `process`]{.slide-title}
::: {#1fd6218f .cell execution_count=8} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Coefficient Std. error t-statistic p-value Intercept 7.6953 0.4421 17.41 < 0.0001 process 0.0460 0.0026 17.64 < 0.0001 ``` ::: ::: . . .
::: {.body-text-m} Each additional \$1K in process upgrade spending is associated with **0\.0460 ktCO₂e** (~46 metric tons) of additional annual CO₂ reduction. ::: --- ## {#checkin-pvalue data-menu-title="Check-in: p-values" background="#053660"}

Check-in

The $p$-value for `process` is < 0.0001. What does this tell us?

The intercept also has a very small $p$-value. Does this mean the intercept is an important predictor of CO₂ reductions?

--- ## {#section-model-accuracy data-menu-title="# Model accuracy #" background="#047C90"}

Assessing model accuracy

--- ## {#rse data-menu-title="Residual Standard Error"} [Residual Standard Error (RSE)]{.slide-title}
The **RSE** estimates the standard deviation of $\epsilon$ — the average amount the response deviates from the true regression line: $$RSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-2} RSS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-2} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$$ . . .
::: {.body-text-m} - A smaller RSE means predictions are closer to observed values - RSE is in the **same units as $Y$** → directly interpretable - "Small" is context-dependent: compare relative to the range or mean of $Y$ ::: --- ## {#checkin-rse data-menu-title="Check-in: RSE" background="#053660"}

Check-in

For the GreenAbate SLR, RSE ≈ 3\.14 ktCO₂e (≈ 3138 metric tons).

Is this a good or bad fit? What additional information would you need to judge this?

--- ## {#r2-def data-menu-title="R² statistic"} [$R^2$ statistic]{.slide-title}
$R^2$ measures the **proportion of variance in $Y$ explained by the model**: $$R^2 = \frac{TSS - RSS}{TSS} = 1 - \frac{RSS}{TSS}$$ where: - $TSS = \sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ — **total** variability in $Y$ - $RSS = \sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ — variability **unexplained** by the regression . . . $$\underbrace{TSS}_{\text{total variability}} - \underbrace{RSS}_{\text{unexplained}} = \underbrace{TSS - RSS}_{\text{explained by regression}}$$ --- ## {#r2-interpret data-menu-title="Interpreting R²"} [Interpreting $R^2$]{.slide-title}

::: {.body-text-m} - $R^2 \in [0, 1]$ always - $R^2$ close to **1** → model explains most of the variability → good fit - $R^2$ close to **0** → $X$ alone is not capturing what drives $Y$ - Could mean: non-linear relationship, more predictors needed, high irreducible noise - What counts as "good" is **context-dependent** ::: . . .
::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text} Always look at your data — a high $R^2$ does not guarantee a good model! ::: --- ## {#accuracy-table data-menu-title="Model accuracy: GreenAbate"} [Model accuracy: `co2_reduction` ~ `process`]{.slide-title}
::: {#7141bbc7 .cell execution_count=9} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Quantity Value Residual standard error 3.14 ktCO₂e R² 0.611 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#e9c14dbb .cell execution_count=10} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-11-output-1.png){width=789 height=337 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: --- ## {#checkin-r2 data-menu-title="Check-in: R²" background="#053660"}

Check-in

$R^2$ = 0\.611 and RSE = 3\.14 ktCO₂e for the SLR on `process`.

How would you interpret $R^2$ in plain language?

Is RSE = 3\.14 a good fit for a firm targeting 5 ktCO₂e/year? What about 25 ktCO₂e/year?
